anchor.snippet: a context-free grammar ( CFG ) is a formal grammar whose production rules can be applied to a nonterminal symbol regardless of its context
A formal grammar is essentially a set of production rules that describe all possible strings in a given formal language.
The language generated by a grammar is the set of all strings of terminal symbols that can be derived
Languages generated by context-free grammars are known as context-free languages (CFL).
do two given context-free grammars generate the same language?) is undecidable

A language L is said to be a context-free language (CFL), if there exists a CFG G
Non-deterministic pushdown automata recognize exactly the context-free languages.

The canonical example of a context-free grammar is parenthesis matching, which is representative of the general case.

The formation rules for the terms and formulas of formal logic fit the definition of context-free grammar

anchor.section: Examples of languages that are not context-free
anchor.snippet: there is no context-free grammar for generating all sequences of two different types of parentheses
Every regular grammar is context-free, but not all context-free grammars are regular.
The following context-free grammar, for example, is also regular.
Every regular grammar corresponds directly to a nondeterministic finite automaton
A derivation of a string for a grammar is a sequence of grammar rule applications that transform the start symbol into the string.
in a leftmost derivation , it is always the leftmost nonterminal; in a rightmost derivation , it is always the rightmost nonterminal.
This tree is called a parse tree or "concrete syntax tree" of the string
then the grammar is said to be an ambiguous grammar .
such languages are called inherently ambiguous languages .
Every context-free grammar with no ε -production has an equivalent grammar in Chomsky normal form , and a grammar in Greibach normal form .
one can use the Chomsky normal form to construct a polynomial-time algorithm that decides whether a given string is in the language
Context-free languages are closed under the various operations
They are not closed under general intersection (hence neither under complementation ) and set difference.
The parsing problem, checking whether a given word belongs to the language given by a context-free grammar, is decidable
Context-free parsing for Chomsky normal form grammars was shown by Leslie G. Valiant to be reducible to Boolean matrix multiplication

A context-free grammar is said to be proper if it has neither useless symbols nor
every context-free grammar not generating ε can be transformed into a weakly equivalent proper one.
It is decidable whether a given grammar is a regular grammar
Given a context-free grammar, it is not decidable whether its language is regular
There are algorithms to decide whether the language of a given context-free grammar is empty, as well as whether it is finite.
is undecidable for context-sensitive grammars , but decidable for context-free grammars.
Given a CFG, does it generate the language of all strings over the alphabet of terminal symbols used in its rules?
Given two CFGs, do they generate the same language?
Given two CFGs, can the first one generate all strings that the second one can generate?

anchor.section: Being in a lower or higher level of the Chomsky hierarchy
anchor.snippet: Using Greibach's theorem , it can be shown that the two following problems are undecidable
Given a CFG, is it ambiguous ?
Given two CFGs, is there any string derivable from both grammars?
An extended context-free grammar (or regular right part grammar ) is one in which the right-hand side of the production rules is allowed to be a regular expression
Extended context-free grammars describe exactly the context-free languages.
This produces the formalism of context-sensitive grammars .
LR( k ) grammars (also known as deterministic context-free grammars ) allow parsing (string recognition) with deterministic pushdown automata
LL( k ) and LL( * ) grammars allow parsing by direct construction of a leftmost derivation as described above
Simple grammars are a subclass of the LL(1) grammars mostly interesting for its theoretical property
Bracketed grammars have the property that the terminal symbols are divided into left and right bracket pairs that always match up in rules.
Linear grammars have no rules with more than one nonterminal on the right-hand side.
Regular grammars are a subclass of the linear grammars and describe the regular languages
